description: Discovery reduces risk before committing to broader rollout. These tracks test whether bike-mobility opportunities are valuable, viable, usable, and feasible before the organization scales them further.

riskFramework.question: Will customers buy the product and/or choose to use it?
Will the solution meet the needs of the business?
Can customers figure out how to use it?
Can we build it within given resources and constraints?

items.title: Partner Service Control Tower: dealer network economics discovery
items.name: Partner Service Control Tower: dealer network economics discovery
items.summary: Discovery work is testing whether a more explicit service-control-tower model improves dealer trust, reduces reimbursement friction, and creates a stronger service-margin foundation across the partner ecosystem.
items.opportunity: Clarify whether stronger dealer & service partner network management, service orchestration, and claims administration create enough partner and business value to justify deeper investment in the Partner Service Control Tower initiative.

linkedInitiatives.description: Partner service control tower initiative focused on improving dealer & service partner network management, stock, availability & handover planning, service case & maintenance orchestration, and insurance & claims administration across Partner Portal and Partner Integration API.

assignments.how: Leads the discovery framing, service-operations evidence gathering, and recommendation on how far to standardize the partner service control tower.
Supports the discovery with dealer workflow detail, coverage constraints, and partner onboarding insights.
Supports the discovery with claims turnaround evidence and reimbursement bottleneck analysis.
Supports the discovery with settlement-cycle data and unit-economics tradeoff analysis.

riskFocus.question: Will customers buy the product and/or choose to use it?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Dealers and service partners will stay more engaged if the platform reduces rework, accelerates reimbursement, and makes service work commercially clearer.
evidenceToCollect: Whether dealers describe reimbursement delay and service ambiguity as top reasons for dissatisfaction.
Whether partners say a clearer service-control-tower model would change their willingness to expand usage.

riskFocus.question: Will the solution meet the needs of the business?
riskFocus.hypothesis: The business can justify deeper investment if lower partner churn and better service economics outweigh the operational cost of a more centralized service model.
evidenceToCollect: What settlement-cycle improvement is required to materially improve partner retention.
Which parts of service orchestration should remain local versus globally standardized.

riskFocus.question: Can customers figure out how to use it?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Partner portal users can follow the new intake, authorization, and reimbursement sequence with less back-and-forth than the current workflow.
evidenceToCollect: Where dealers and claims handlers hesitate in prototype flows.
Which workflow states remain unclear across partner, claims, and finance teams.

riskFocus.question: Can we build it within given resources and constraints?
riskFocus.hypothesis: The teams can deliver the smallest useful service-control-tower slice without creating a disruptive migration burden for current partners.
evidenceToCollect: Which policy, payout, and partner-system dependencies block the new workflow.
Whether the rollout can be sliced by claim type, partner cohort, or country.

plannedActivities: Interview dealer operators and workshop managers in priority regions about payout friction and service workflow pain.
Replay recent claims and maintenance cases to identify the slowest approval and reimbursement steps.
Prototype a simplified partner service timeline that combines service, claims, and settlement status.
items.decisionToMake: Decide whether to expand the service-control-tower model into a broader cross-market operating pattern or keep it limited to high-friction partner segments.

items.title: Fleet expansion model: pooled bike operations discovery
items.name: Fleet expansion model: pooled bike operations discovery
items.summary: Discovery work is testing whether pooled-bike operations can become a repeatable product line beyond pilots by validating utilization, operational fit, and expansion economics.
items.opportunity: Clarify whether stronger pool bike & fleet management, trip monitoring, and fleet analytics create enough value to scale beyond pilot campuses and field-operations use cases.

linkedInitiatives.description: Fleet mobility pilot initiative focused on improving pool bike & fleet management, trip, telematics & asset monitoring, service case & maintenance orchestration, and operational data, KPI & analytics platform across Fleet Admin Console and Fleet User Mobile Experience.

assignments.how: Leads the fleet opportunity framing, customer learning plan, and recommendation on which operational fleet segments merit scaling.
Supports the discovery with maintenance-readiness evidence and operational constraints from pooled assets.
Supports the discovery with utilization, downtime, and task-substitution measurement design.
Supports the discovery with telemetry, platform-dependability, and rollout-feasibility analysis.

riskFocus.question: Will customers buy the product and/or choose to use it?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Corporate fleet operators will expand beyond pilots if pooled bikes reliably replace real short-distance operational trips.
evidenceToCollect: Whether operators report enough trip substitution and asset usage to justify broader rollout.
Which fleet use cases value bikes most: campuses, hospitality, field service, or urban logistics.

riskFocus.question: Will the solution meet the needs of the business?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Fleet mobility becomes viable if service, theft, and downtime costs stay within a predictable range at modest utilization levels.
evidenceToCollect: What rides-per-asset threshold creates acceptable economics.
How much operational support is required per site once the fleet is live.

riskFocus.question: Can customers figure out how to use it?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Operational users can book, access, and report issues on pooled bikes without requiring heavy local admin intervention.
evidenceToCollect: Where site managers or users fail in booking, checkout, or issue reporting workflows.
Which access and assignment rules are confusing in real operations.

riskFocus.question: Can we build it within given resources and constraints?
riskFocus.hypothesis: The current platform and service model can support a small number of repeatable fleet patterns without creating custom one-off deployments for every site.
evidenceToCollect: Which telematics, access, and maintenance dependencies block a standardized offer.
Whether rollout can be packaged into a limited set of fleet templates.

plannedActivities: Observe live pilot usage at one campus-style site and one field-operations site.
Model utilization and downtime scenarios required for healthy fleet economics.
Define two or three repeatable fleet templates rather than continuing with bespoke pilots.
items.decisionToMake: Decide whether pooled fleet mobility should remain a narrow pilot offer or become a formal second growth line with standardized rollout patterns.
